Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd usually just wait until I felt like studying or when something was due soon. With my extracurriculars taking up a lot of time, I'd squeeze in studying whenever I could, often late at night when I'd listen to lecture recordings. It was pretty scattered, and I know I could've been more organized, but somehow it worked out okay for my grades.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything academic, and then I'd have these bursts of activity when deadlines loomed. I'm the type who responds to pressure, so regular scheduling never really stuck with me. My extracurriculars and social stuff usually took priority until assignments became urgent.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about a deadline or just boredom scrolling through my phone that made me check the LMS. Sometimes I'd log in because friends mentioned something about the course in our group chat. I wasn't proactive about it at all—I needed some kind of external push to actually open the platform.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. It never became a natural habit for me. The closest it got was when they introduced those gamified tasks—the points and badges actually made me curious enough to check in more often. But even then, it was more like a game I'd play occasionally rather than a study routine. I'd forget about it for days and then binge-check everything.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I could play them while doing other things or lying in bed, which fit my lazy approach perfectly. The discussion forums were also helpful because I could see what confused other people and figure out if I was missing something important. Those gamified challenges actually made some boring content more tolerable too.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. Reading just doesn't stick for me the way listening does, so I'd download them and never open them again. The lengthy discussion threads also got skipped unless they were directly related to an assignment. I figured if something was really important, it would come up in the videos anyway.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was either directly tied to my grade or actually interesting enough to hold my attention. The gamified stuff worked because it made me feel like I was accomplishing something even if the content was dry. If something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I'd skip it without guilt. I need to see the point immediately or I just drift away.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist in my mind unless they had some incentive attached. Required stuff I'd eventually do because I had to, but optional things needed to be genuinely fun or offer bonus points. The gamified optional tasks were the exception—those badges and leaderboards actually got me to do extra work sometimes. Without that hook, optional meant ignorable.
Q9: Yeah, I did a lot of studying outside the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it while commuting or working out. My friends and I would also have study sessions where we'd explain concepts to each other verbally. I used YouTube videos to understand difficult topics because sometimes random creators explain things better than professors. None of that showed up anywhere in my course activity.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely learning but the LMS wouldn't know. Like when I'd replay downloaded lectures multiple times while doing chores, or when I'd have long conversations with classmates about course material over coffee. Sometimes I'd think through problems in my head during my commute without any materials in front of me. The LMS only sees clicks, not actual understanding happening.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access a lecture video once, download the audio, and then listen to it multiple times offline over the following days. Same with some PowerPoint slides—I'd screenshot the important ones and review them on my phone later. The LMS would show one access, but I might have engaged with that content five or six more times without logging back in.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when my anxiety would spike even though I was generally in a good mood otherwise. The combination of multiple deadlines from different courses and extracurricular commitments made everything feel overwhelming. I'd log in, see all the pending tasks, feel paralyzed, and just close the tab. Those were the weeks my engagement really dropped.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed about other things, the LMS became this thing I'd avoid because it reminded me of all my incomplete work. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars definitely cut into my study time. Sometimes I'd be so mentally exhausted that I'd log in, stare at the screen for a few minutes, and then give up without actually doing anything productive.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the lecture playing but be scrolling through social media on my phone, barely absorbing anything. Or I'd click through materials just to mark them as complete without really reading them. Sometimes I'd be physically present in the LMS but mentally checked out completely. Those sessions were basically wasted time that looked productive on paper.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was anxiety—the task felt so big that starting seemed impossible, so I'd avoid it until the pressure became unbearable. I'd tell myself I work better under pressure anyway, which is sometimes true but mostly just an excuse. The gamified tasks were easier to start because they felt lower stakes.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the 24-48 hours before something was due. I'd cram everything into those last-minute sessions, which is why my response time averaged almost a full day. Without a deadline, I had zero motivation to engage with any material, no matter how important it was supposed to be.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but in complicated ways. Good feedback made me feel happy but didn't really change my habits. Critical feedback would sometimes trigger my anxiety and make me avoid the course for a few days. The gamified instant feedback was actually the most motivating because it was immediate and felt less personal. Waiting for feedback on assignments just made me anxious and disengaged.
Q18: The course structure was okay but didn't really match how I learn. Having everything laid out with clear modules helped me see what needed to be done, but it also made the workload feel overwhelming when I fell behind. The gamified elements helped break things into smaller chunks that felt more manageable. Weekly deadlines were better than having everything due at the end because they forced me to engage somewhat regularly.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they gave me small wins that kept me coming back even when I wasn't feeling motivated. On the harder side, the sheer volume of reading materials was discouraging since that's not how I learn best. Having more audio content or interactive elements would have helped me stay engaged. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, which made them feel like chores rather than learning opportunities.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or overwhelmed—they keep checking the LMS hoping to feel prepared but can't bring themselves to actually complete the work. It might also be people who are exploring or trying to understand what's expected without committing to submissions. Or honestly, sometimes you're just clicking around procrastinating while telling yourself you're studying. The activity looks productive but nothing's actually getting done.
Q21: That kind of describes me somewhat. I didn't log in that often, but when I did engage, I was usually pretty focused. Plus, all my offline studying—listening to lectures, discussing with friends—contributed to my understanding without showing up in the data. You can learn a lot without being constantly visible in the system. Sometimes quality of engagement matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you access, which reveals something about habits and deadline responses. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually thinking or just clicking. They can't capture offline learning, peer discussions, or mental processing that happens away from the screen. They also can't tell if you're anxious, happy, or completely zoned out while technically being 'active.'
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to complete requirements. It's when I'm genuinely curious about a topic and want to learn more, not just earn points or avoid failing. Real engagement feels different—there's energy and interest there. Unfortunately, I didn't feel that way about most of the course content, even when I was technically participating.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my internal struggle with anxiety that made simple tasks feel impossible sometimes. It wouldn't capture how the gamified elements actually helped my mood and made me more willing to engage. It also misses all the learning I did through conversations with friends and listening to lectures offline. Most importantly, it can't show that despite appearing disengaged, I actually cared about doing well—I just had trouble translating that into consistent visible action.
